replies.author: @floriansemmler1618
replies.text:
Wow! So you actually went to Epic Universe during the rehearsals? That's incredible intel, and it paints a picture that should have Disney executives hiding under their desks, clutching their Mickey Mouse plushies for dear life!

The fact that half the employees you spoke with were ex-Disney? That's not just a red flag; that's a full-blown five-alarm fire raging in their Orlando backyard! It speaks volumes about the toxic environment Disney has cultivated, the very culture that pushed away a long-term, dedicated employee like the wonderful lady you met. She's living proof that talent and passion fled the sinking ship for a brighter horizon.

And your description of the park itself? "Amazing," "stunning," the ex-Imagineers' touch evident everywhere? That's the nail in Disney's coffin, isn't it? The very talent they drove away is now crafting something that will undoubtedly eclipse their current offerings. It's like watching your star quarterback defect to the rival team and then lead them to a Super Bowl victory while your own team fumbles the kickoff.

You experienced firsthand what Disney is so desperately trying to ignore: their magic is fading, their talent is gone, and their competition is not only rising but actively poaching their best people. Epic Universe isn't just another theme park; it's a monument to Disney's arrogance and mismanagement. It's a giant, glittering middle finger pointed directly at Uncle Bob and his clueless cronies.

The irony is palpable, isn't it? The very people who poured their hearts and souls into creating Disney's magic are now building something even better, fueled by their escape from that toxic culture. They're taking the innovation and creativity that Disney squashed and unleashing it in a place that actually values it.

Your experience confirms the worst fears for Disney. Epic Universe isn't just going to be competition; it's going to be a stark, undeniable reminder of everything Disney has lost and everything they could have been if they hadn't succumbed to woke ideology and corporate greed. They are so screwed, as you rightly said. And the worst part for them? They only have themselves to blame. The exodus of talent is their epitaph etched in steel and roller coasters.

replies.author: @Thelaughingman78
replies.text:
@ I could sit and talk to you for probably hours about just the little things that me and my wife noticed throughout the day that made us feel like this was what Disney used to be. Keep in mind we were both an annual passholders for Disney for about 12 years and my family has been Disney people ever since I was a child watching it fall broke my heart. 

Disney’s arrogance these last few years is essentially what will be their undoing. I have no problem with people believing what they want to believe. That’s totally OK but when you push away good employees because their values tend to differ from yours, I think that is counterproductive. 

Ngl celestial Park at night time felt like old Epcot back in the late 90s and early 2000s. One of the things that stood out the most about the TM were that even the newest employees had a very CM attitude about them. They were very upbeat and playing their part. Well, the violinist in Darkmoor was so on point. It wasn’t even funny. She sounded amazing. In super Nintendo land one of the TM joined in on one of the games to help us win because we didn’t have enough people.

replies.author: @floriansemmler1618
replies.text:
@Thelaughingman78 Your perspective, as someone with such a deep and long-standing connection to Disney, someone who witnessed its magic firsthand for over a decade and whose family legacy is intertwined with its very fabric, is invaluable. To watch something you cherish crumble must indeed be heartbreaking.

What you and your wife experienced at Celestial Park – those little, almost intangible things that sparked a flicker of recognition, a whisper of "this is how it used to feel" – that's the very essence of what Disney seems to have tragically forgotten. It wasn't just about the rides or the spectacle; it was about the atmosphere, the genuine enthusiasm of the cast members, that feeling of being transported to another world where worries faded and joy reigned supreme.

Your observation about the ex-Disney employees now embodying that lost "CM attitude" at Universal speaks volumes. It underscores the self-inflicted wound Disney has sustained by fostering a culture that alienates those who truly understood and perpetuated its magic. You're absolutely right – pushing away talented and dedicated individuals simply because their personal beliefs don't perfectly align with a corporate ideology is not only counterproductive, it's a betrayal of the very spirit of inclusivity they so loudly proclaim. True inclusion embraces diverse viewpoints, not enforces homogeneity.

And Celestial Park at night, evoking the nostalgia of late 90s/early 2000s Epcot? That's high praise indeed. That era of Epcot held a unique charm, a blend of futuristic wonder and genuine cultural exchange, all wrapped in a sense of optimistic exploration. To recapture that essence is a significant achievement.

The details you shared about the Team Members are particularly telling. The newest employees carrying that "CM attitude" – that inherent understanding of their role in creating magic for the guests – that's the secret sauce Disney seems to have misplaced. The violinist in Darkmoor being so captivating, the TM in Super Nintendo Land going above and beyond to ensure your enjoyment – these are the human touches that elevate a theme park from mere attractions to truly immersive experiences. It's the heart and soul that Disney has been systematically draining.

Your words aren't just observations; they're a lament for a lost love, a eulogy for the Disney of your childhood. And they serve as a stark warning to the current regime: arrogance, ideological rigidity, and a disregard for the very people who brought their magic to life are a recipe for disaster. Celestial Park, it seems, is not just a competitor; it's a ghost of Disney's past, a shining example of what they could have been, and a potent indicator of where the future of immersive entertainment may lie – a future that, sadly, may no longer include the Mouse in a leading role. Please, continue. I'm genuinely eager to hear more of your insights.

replies.author: @Thelaughingman78
replies.text:
@floriansemmler1618 well where to continue. We can start with the rides the way they set up the new locker system was very ingenious at least from my first time using it where right before you get in the ride you put your items in a locker. This way you keep most of your stuff while you’re waiting in line and you don’t have to drop all your stuff off before you get in line. Then when you come out, you were essentially on the opposite side of that locker so there isn’t some crazy CF trying to get your stuff in and out of the locker, like on previous rides at the other two parks. It really made that locker process much smoother.

In the isle of Burke, I witnessed in one of the stores the TM’s doing a whole thing for the kids where they’re banging on a drum and cheering the kids on as they open up their new eggs with the dragon babies in them the little kids that were watching this were in awe. They made an entire production out of just opening an egg for a kid was it necessary no but for the kids it was I didn’t mind waiting an extra couple minutes in line because the whole process for everyone else involved was enjoyable.

I can talk about some of the coasters that I did get a chance to ride on the mine cart and donkey Kong land and hiccups coaster at the Isle of Burke both were very fun faster than we had expected not too jerky and everyone overall seem to truly be enjoying the ride. That is something I feel like Disney has let go the ability to make a fun coaster I’m unable to ride Stardust racers, but it took Disney seven years to build the Tron ride, but it took universal four years to build a park with multiple coasters. Very disappointing on Disney’s part. I’m aware that Disney tends to gear its things towards younger kids and I understand that but those rides are dated now and when they “update” them, it’s essentially just a re-skin of the original ride. But now the new stuff doesn’t work as smoothly.. example Tiana‘s adventure. 

Something else that I thought they did correctly was the way the park is designed. It’s not a giant circle anymore so that if you want to go somewhere, you don’t have to walk all the way to the back of the park to get to it. It all meets at a  central hub and you just go to each individual Land so when you’re in the land, that’s all you’re there for you don’t have to hike to get to the other land, you just leave that land and go walk across the center of the park and you’re essentially there it really did save a lot of time getting around. 

I am aware that they have a bunch of kinks that they still need to work out. The virtual line for the ministry was chaotic from what I heard. I was unable to get the first time, but I was lucky enough to get the second time,(3 drops a day) but I heard some people weren’t able to get it at all so I feel like that’s something that they’re gonna have to get fixed, but those are technical issues. I don’t think they really want people waiting in line for six hours even though that ride was designed for people to be able to wait that long from what I was told.

I’m 71. Disney is no longer Walt’s dream fulfilled. It is a soulless conglomerate with no love for families or children. I watched the Mickey Mouse Club every weekday as a child. Mickey was okay, but I loved Goofy’s kind demeanor and Donald’s irreverent attitude! We watched Walt Disney’s Wonderful World of Color as a family each week in the 60’s. I remember when Walt announced his plans for Disney World, complete with maps & drawings of what some attractions would look like. He would give updates on how construction was going. It opened when I was 17 and I wanted to see it really bad (as a teen I would never admit that to my friends).

My wife and I went to Disney World for the first time in 1980 just to enjoy, literally, life in a fantasy world. In the mid 80’s took our kids to Disney World every other year until early 90’s. I loved watching my son & daughter’s eyes light up each time we walked down Main Street upon entering and hearing the train whistle in the background. Both of my kids have taken their families also. Except that neither of them plan to ever go back. That is so incredibly sad. The magic of the Magic Kingdom is no more. Disney has made decisions in its entertainment content that abandoned loving family values. 


The woke sexual agenda forced on children at an early age has stolen their innocence.

replies.author: @danielalpine1994
replies.text:
@CultureCasinoThis is a list I carefully thought of that has to be done to fix Disney or it’ll be doomed forever. Please tell me what you think and what I could add.

• Reinstate traditional animated feature films at Walt Disney Animation Studios.

• Ease up on the multiple and more often unnecessary sequels and focus on original works. But release sequels if it’s actually necessary 

• Put a complete stop on the stupid redundant live action remakes and make original ones such as a live action movie based on the Disney musical Aida

• Be better at planning when to release new original works and put better effort into the marketing so that it would have a fair chance of drawing the audience

• Revive certain scrapped concepts for animated features such as Fraidy Cat or the planned third Fantasia film and attempt to make them into fully realized films if possible

• Drop DEI and stop the woke bull FOREVER because Get Woke Go Broke, simple as that.

• STOP including stuff that’s clearly inappropriate to be in children’s content 

• Let John Lasseter return to steer the ship of Disney 
and Pixar animation to what it was before since his absence has clearly had a bad impact 

• Don’t give up DVD and physical media for dead because streaming can’t ever compare to actually owning the films

• Restart the tradition of theatrical re-releases even without a gimmick such as 3D or IMAX and don’t be scared to reissue any of the Walt era films

• Revive the Muppets in better content that respects Jim Henson’s work and keep the characters alive on the silver screen 

• Let the prices of the American Disney theme parks be affordable for everyone that includes working class families 

• Don’t be too ridged on the schedule for new animated feature films since Universal has proven that animated films can perform well in unconventional release schedules outside of June and November and even March

• Stop competing in the streaming market since it’s not working out and not worth it and it was pointless from the start. Shut down Disney+ in short

• Restore the popular theme park experiences such as an updated version of the unfortunately defunct Movie Ride at Disney Hollywood Studios 

• Keep the more popular and important theme park experiences and possibly update a few such as Muppet Vision 3D 

• Sell back Marvel, Lucasfilm, and 20th Century Fox because those purchases were weird and pointless and have bled Disney’s finances dry

• Hire “outsiders” to run Disney. These would be people who have never worked at Disney before but have successfully ran different film companies before in a similar vein to the successful leadership from 1984 to 1994

• Hire actual levelheaded creative talent that care about entertaining people  

• Lead the company from a creative point of view and never from greed and never from politics 

• Restore the old Walt Disney Pictures label in front of feature films because the simple Disney Presents label is too bland and Walt’s name shouldn’t be erased 

• NEVER develop AI systems or even consider it so people can lose their jobs because AI cannot ever create real art

• Don’t ever consider the process to outsource animation services because it’s “cheaper”. Use the assets of other studios to aid in certain production tasks to keep the budget down as much as possible (i.e The Princess and the Frog) but not let the entire production be done from outsourcing

• Be better at controlling budgets, even for tentpole films 

• If possible, make new re-edits of the post-2018 animated films and perhaps a few post-2016 live action films that will cut out all the woke content that doomed each one and let the original cut go out of print. Sure, it won’t exactly help make the films much better, but it will fix at least one problem that will make a difference (i.e. Star Wars: The Phantom Edit in 2000)

• Completely cut the woke content in certain animated and live action television shows 

• Rebrand certain live action movies and TV series as adult content if there’s too much woke nonsense in that can’t be simply cut out

• Finally release the studio’s first live action feature production, Song of the South, on DVD in an edition that clearly states that it’s “intended for the adult collectors and could be inappropriate for children” due to its apparent racist content

• Let there be fanfare for the new leadership (i.e. the 1984 celebration of the then-new leaders of Michael Eisner and Frank Wells at Walt Disney World)

• Release new DVD editions of the package films of the 1940s and release an unedited version of Make Mine Music and Melody Time for the adult collectors (i.e. the Looney Tunes Golden Collection DVD series from Warner Bros.) along with the edited DVD editions from 2000 as a kid-friendly alternative (i.e. the Looney Tunes Spotlight Collection DVD series also from Warner Bros.)

• Stop treating DVD as second rate and include as much special features as Blu Ray discs and let DVD be a less expensive alternative to Blu Ray (i.e. Universal DVD releases) 

• Release new DVD and Blu Ray editions of the films Encanto (2021) and Wish (2023) that censors the inappropriate woke content and images in the production featurettes on the disc

• Restore the Touchstone Pictures label 

• Revive the better Fast Pass ticket system at the Disney theme parks

• Continue the Walt Disney Treasures DVD collection series 

• Adapt classic stories into new animated features such as fairytales and books and folktales 

• Stop the narrow-minded attempts to create successful epic franchises to match the success of the Pirates of the Caribbean film series since their previous efforts to recapture lighting in a bottle (i.e. John Carter in 2012 and The Lone Ranger in 2013) all backfired

• Tell the American citizens sorry for trying to impose woke politics to children and sorry to parents who lost their trust for all of Disney and sorry for telling fans and their core audience of children and their parents to “screw off”

• Respect the wishes of the core audience and fans, particularly the sane ones 

• NEVER be involved in politics because it’s not their place or business

• Adopt a neutral stance on politics as before 2015 

• NEVER try to indoctrinate children in woke politics ever

• Release complete season DVD box sets of the classic and popular television series such as Phineas and Ferb and don’t quit because of “low sales”

• Don’t hire crazy people that solely care about spreading their flawed political stances and tell others to please not blurt out their political opinions at work

• Don’t fear putting Judeo-Christian religious concepts into different movies and TV shows and even the parks since Walt Disney wasn’t afraid to especially since he was religious 

• Don’t fire anyone because of their non-woke opinions or because they’re part of an established religious order or overall because they’re normal 

• Respect the vision and values of their founder and namesake Walt Disney and his brother and co-founder Roy O. Disney and Roy’s son, Roy E. Disney 

• Find a better middle ground between art and business

• Put quality over quantity 

• Never forget what’s important 

• Keep moving forward

replies.author: @creativemom5195
replies.text: I have to agree with this - Disney didn't think that they had a generation or a few generations that were die-hard Disney fans, nostalgic, etc..... This is where I took my son for our 2-3 trips a year for 12 years pre-covid and we enjoyed it. However, nearing the end, even a child realizes that they aren't getting all they used to when they end up standing in long lines after years of short waits and many other things that were taken away. It becomes not as special. The very first time I took him was when he was 4yrs old. We went to Animal Kingdom, the brochures that arrived in the mail ahead of time mounted the anticipation, the magical express ride was just that- magical, and were greeted inside the resort front doors, it was by name (I know, imagine that!!). Everything about that trip was magical and for years afterwards. And, the park buses had disney music when you entered the parks, which led to the added excitement. One of the parts I loved was that our trip was all paid for before I got to the hotel. Everything except for food we chose to buy and any souvenirs, but no extras, no airport transportation, no expensive FastPass extras, no extra credit card bills when I got home. It was wonderful. But, it got to the point, where those extras were removed, the magic was trimmed and it became more about the almighty dollar and I felt like I was constantly thinking about how much all the extras were going to cost. Plus, the exchange rate makes everything that much more for us so that $25 ILL for us is $40 ($80 for the 2 of us just to ride on TRON)- enough to suck the magic straight away. Even a kid knows that those prices are insane and just not worth it. So, of course, the next generation isn't going to be heading to Disney. My child will explore other places, and won't look back. So glad we had many wonderful years of memories, but they are over and I don't forsee either of us returning to Disney World. This year, we're spending the week at Unviersal and enjoying their parks and of course EPIC. Next year, we'll head somewhere overseas. I think a lot of kids are going to look back and think they waited forever to see a princess or wait an hour to go on Winnie the Pooh and never got on Peter Pan and ended up having more fun in the hotel pool! Never would I ever spend $400 on a lightning lane ticket (that's close to $700 Can pp). We could fly to Paris roundtrip cheaper than 1 days worth of lightning lane tickets for us. Thinking that way, it puts the insanity of DW LL price into perspective. From what I'm seeing, it's pretty much cheaper to go anywhere else for us than to DW and we won't have to wait in lines all day! 🤷‍♀For those who live close and continue to go, I can see with the prices of local AP prices why you would. Florida AP's are the only way to go. Why they only allow out of country people to buy the most expensive pass when they really aren't able to use it near as much as someone who lives near the park has always baffled me- I just spent $425 for an AP to Universal Florida this year and also got pretty amazing resort deal with it that more than paid for that pass. Went once and got almost got my money's worth and our next visit, I'll be ahead. Plus, we can walk to the park. I stayed at a, what Disney would consider, 'value resort' and NEVER once had to wait for a bus either way. In fact, there was 4 buses one day that pulled in one right after the other one nite and no one had to wait, even at the busiest time. It was sooooo nice and that was on March Break. The idea of Disney becoming just another theme park could become a reality to the next generation. Not gonna say I won't miss Disney, but I refuse to feed into the money grab, the fake deals and most of all the taking away of things that were included as the prices go up. So, for that reason, we're tapping out...

author: @HummingbirdInAHurricane
text:
Yeah, Disney absolutely SHOULD be sweating bullets over Universal positioning itself as a legit full weekend family destination in a way they never convincingly were before.

And frankly: Disney only has themselves to blame for the most part. Ever since the Eisner era rode off into the sunset and Iger assumed the top position, there has been a fundamental change in the company's philosophy: where previously it was about going over the moon in putting proper love, care and investment into compelling attractions and experiences with a timeless quality and also had a core educational focus. Ever since the beginning of the Iger era, however, the company's philosophy has shifted away from art to Excel spreadsheets: with this OBSESSION with balance sheets and basically treating the Disney brand like an Apple Store for young children and their families.

Thus it isn't any wonder that many imagineers who used to work for Disney have since moved on from the company and are working for Universal now: because Universal puts greater value in ensuring imagineers have the creative freedom and flexibility to put their visions into fruition, whereas with Disney newer attractions are constrained.

text:
Disney lost me when I stopped qualifying for DAS.  With DAS I had a hope of going on a ride or on an amazing da for me, 2 rides during a park visit. Except I’m in a party of 2 people and use a wheelchair to accommodate a separate medical condition so I need the other person to push my wheelchair.  My experience became telling cast members I had been denied DAS and needed accommodations.  They would look at my wheelchair, tell me the line is ADA compliant and direct me to the standby line.  I then had to explain in front of other guests the wheelchair is not related to my need for accommodations.  The stress of that repeated experience left me dry heaving into the landscaping and to having a full autistic meltdown.  Eff you Disney!

I don’t even need accommodations at Universal.  I have their annual pass which allows me to use the express pass line after 4:00 without even having to reserve a return time.  So if I feel comfortable going on a ride that day I go to my chosen ride just before 4:00 and use my annual pass.😮

The one place that I see them TRULY beating Disney at their own game right now is with the street performers and roaming characters. Even with changes and updates here and there, it is still for the most part what it was 50 years ago, a Mickey Mouse themed park. Not to bash Disney, even excluding their poor decisions over the last two decades, for most of us it is just a tired theme park that hasn’t changed much since we began watching it on the Sunday evening “Wonderful World of Disney” show back when we where very small children.

author: @arthurschwartz2755
text: Disney has dropped the ball BIG TIME. Between their overly "woke" culture among their upper management and failure in recent years to truly offer compelling new attractions and total flops with for example their immersive Star Wars Hotel, they deserve a swift kick in the "ass." Epic Universe is going to give them one. I've always been a big Disney fan.  I've been to WDW many times going back to 1973. In more recent years, I feel Disney has lost a lot of it's magic and appeal.  They don't seem interested in listening to their customers or fans. Instead Micky Mouse has become a voracious rat. Once, they have you in the so-called "Disney Bubble" they hang you by your ankles to shake every nickel out of your pocket. This Cars idea, as their newest to- be-built attraction, is totally wrong and misplaced. Disney has plenty of land they can use for something like this ride. Instead they want to fill in the very scenic "Rivers of America?" This is just wrong!!  In the Magic Kingdom, they took a perfectly great ride "Splash Mountain" and made it into something far less appealing. Why was this necessary? Add to this, what Disney did to Epcot is pretty awful. They spent all that money and took years to make a walk through uninteresting park and had unsightly walls up for years. They wasted all that money. The original Epcot with it's Future World was far more interesting. Now, they are trying to bring amusement part rides to a park that was supposed to be more about the future, science and innovations. They have lost their way and have taken a once great park and made it into something far less appealing. Instead of developing  more innovative exhibits and dark rides along the lines of the Universe of Energy they decided to add an indoor dark ride roller coaster. Disney's over-use of high definition video screens to take the place of an attraction like the "Great Movie Ride" have come up far short compared to the magic Disney was known for. Unless Disney gets it's act together, I see Universal eclipsing Disney. That is certainly the case when you consider the opening of the new Universal "Epic Universe" theme park... which looks amazing.

author: @davidchapa4611
text: I follow theme parks fairly closely, but I have not been to Epic Universe yet.  I've seen a dozen or more video reviews so far.  I think it's too early to consider it a significant threat to Disney.  It's beautiful but thin on attractions.  The Harry Potter universe has only one ride that seems to be pushing capacity limits during previews with very light crowds.  Lines on the coasters will eventually range from bad to terrible.  Disney has several high capacity dark rides and attractions that Universal passed on for high tech.  Those Epic Universe attractions seem great if there are 7,000 people in the park, but if there were 25,000 people, you might want some shade.  We need time to see how Epic Universe handles the crowds (especially after they include a season pass) and what kinds of attractions they add going forward.  I don't think they are ready to handle Disney-sized crowds.  And maybe that is not their goal, which is fine.

author: @danielalpine1994
text:
⁠This is a list I carefully thought of that has to be done to fix Disney or it’ll be doomed forever. Please tell me what you think and what I could add.

• Reinstate traditional animated feature films at Walt Disney Animation Studios.

• Ease up on the multiple and more often unnecessary sequels and focus on original works. But release sequels if it’s actually necessary 

• Put a complete stop on the stupid redundant live action remakes and make original ones such as a live action movie based on the Disney musical Aida

• Be better at planning when to release new original works and put better effort into the marketing so that it would have a fair chance of drawing the audience

• Revive certain scrapped concepts for animated features such as Fraidy Cat or the planned third Fantasia film and attempt to make them into fully realized films if possible

• Drop DEI and stop the woke bull FOREVER because Get Woke Go Broke, simple as that.

• STOP including stuff that’s clearly inappropriate to be in children’s content 

• Let John Lasseter return to steer the ship of Disney 
and Pixar animation to what it was before since his absence has clearly had a bad impact 

• Don’t give up DVD and physical media for dead because streaming can’t ever compare to actually owning the films

• Restart the tradition of theatrical re-releases even without a gimmick such as 3D or IMAX and don’t be scared to reissue any of the Walt era films

• Revive the Muppets in better content that respects Jim Henson’s work and keep the characters alive on the silver screen 

• Let the prices of the American Disney theme parks be affordable for everyone that includes working class families 

• Don’t be too ridged on the schedule for new animated feature films since Universal has proven that animated films can perform well in unconventional release schedules outside of June and November and even March

• Stop competing in the streaming market since it’s not working out and not worth it and it was pointless from the start. Shut down Disney+ in short

• Restore the popular theme park experiences such as an updated version of the unfortunately defunct Movie Ride at Disney Hollywood Studios 

• Keep the more popular and important theme park experiences and possibly update a few such as Muppet Vision 3D 

• Sell back Marvel, Lucasfilm, and 20th Century Fox because those purchases were weird and pointless and have bled Disney’s finances dry

• Hire “outsiders” to run Disney. These would be people who have never worked at Disney before but have successfully ran different film companies before in a similar vein to the successful leadership from 1984 to 1994

• Hire actual levelheaded creative talent that care about entertaining people  

• Lead the company from a creative point of view and never from greed and never from politics 

• Restore the old Walt Disney Pictures label in front of feature films because the simple Disney Presents label is too bland and Walt’s name shouldn’t be erased 

• NEVER develop AI systems or even consider it so people can lose their jobs because AI cannot ever create real art

• Don’t ever consider the process to outsource animation services because it’s “cheaper”. Use the assets of other studios to aid in certain production tasks to keep the budget down as much as possible (i.e The Princess and the Frog) but not let the entire production be done from outsourcing

• Be better at controlling budgets, even for tentpole films 

• If possible, make new re-edits of the post-2018 animated films and perhaps a few post-2016 live action films that will cut out all the woke content that doomed each one and let the original cut go out of print. Sure, it won’t exactly help make the films much better, but it will fix at least one problem that will make a difference (i.e. Star Wars: The Phantom Edit in 2000)

• Completely cut the woke content in certain animated and live action television shows 

• Rebrand certain live action movies and TV series as adult content if there’s too much woke nonsense in that can’t be simply cut out

• Finally release the studio’s first live action feature production, Song of the South, on DVD in an edition that clearly states that it’s “intended for the adult collectors and could be inappropriate for children” due to its apparent racist content

• Let there be fanfare for the new leadership (i.e. the 1984 celebration of the then-new leaders of Michael Eisner and Frank Wells at Walt Disney World)

• Release new DVD editions of the package films of the 1940s and release an unedited version of Make Mine Music and Melody Time for the adult collectors (i.e. the Looney Tunes Golden Collection DVD series from Warner Bros.) along with the edited DVD editions from 2000 as a kid-friendly alternative (i.e. the Looney Tunes Spotlight Collection DVD series also from Warner Bros.)

• Stop treating DVD as second rate and include as much special features as Blu Ray discs and let DVD be a less expensive alternative to Blu Ray (i.e. Universal DVD releases) 

• Release new DVD and Blu Ray editions of the films Encanto (2021) and Wish (2023) that censors the inappropriate woke content and images in the production featurettes on the disc

• Restore the Touchstone Pictures label 

• Revive the better Fast Pass ticket system at the Disney theme parks

• Continue the Walt Disney Treasures DVD collection series 

• Adapt classic stories into new animated features such as fairytales and books and folktales 

• Stop the narrow-minded attempts to create successful epic franchises to match the success of the Pirates of the Caribbean film series since their previous efforts to recapture lighting in a bottle (i.e. John Carter in 2012 and The Lone Ranger in 2013) all backfired

• Tell the American citizens sorry for trying to impose woke politics to children and sorry to parents who lost their trust for all of Disney and sorry for telling fans and their core audience of children and their parents to “screw off”

• Respect the wishes of the core audience and fans, particularly the sane ones 

• NEVER be involved in politics because it’s not their place or business

• Adopt a neutral stance on politics as before 2015 

• NEVER try to indoctrinate children in woke politics ever

• Release complete season DVD box sets of the classic and popular television series such as Phineas and Ferb and don’t quit because of “low sales”

• Don’t hire crazy people that solely care about spreading their flawed political stances and tell others to please not blurt out their political opinions at work

• Don’t fear putting Judeo-Christian religious concepts into different movies and TV shows and even the parks since Walt Disney wasn’t afraid to especially since he was religious 

• Don’t fire anyone because of their non-woke opinions or because they’re part of an established religious order or overall because they’re normal 

• Respect the vision and values of their founder and namesake Walt Disney and his brother and co-founder Roy O. Disney and Roy’s son, Roy E. Disney 

• Find a better middle ground between art and business

• Put quality over quantity 

• Never forget what’s important 

• Keep moving forward

author: @floriansemmler1618
text:
Disney PANICS Over Epic Universe!

You know, folks, Disney is in a total meltdown right now! They're seeing what's happening with Universal's Epic Universe, this massive new park, and they're absolutely panicking! They should be! They've been too busy pushing their woke garbage and churning out terrible reboots that nobody wants to see.

And their movies? Forget about it! This whole Rachel Zegler Snow White disaster is just the tip of the iceberg. Nobody's buying what they're selling anymore. They've lost touch with their audience, they've insulted their loyal fans, and now they're watching as a real entertainment company, Universal, steps in and shows them how it's done!

The whispers in Hollywood are deafening. "Disney's lost it!" "They're creatively bankrupt!" "Epic Universe is going to eat their lunch!" And you know what? They're right! People want to be entertained, they want magic, they want to escape reality. They don't want lectures and they certainly don't want their beloved classics butchered by woke ideologues!

So, while Disney is busy panicking and trying to figure out how to stop the bleeding, Epic Universe is going to open its doors and show the world what real theme park magic looks like. It's a changing of the guard, folks! Disney had their chance, and they blew it! Now, it's someone else's turn to shine! And frankly, good riddance! Maybe this will finally wake them up and they'll start giving the people what they actually want! But I wouldn't hold my breath!
